coredata.dc:title: Amidoximated fluorescent polymer based sensor for detection of trace uranyl ion in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Jiaqi
He, Weiwei
Han, Xiaoli
Hua, Daoben
coredata.dc:description: It is of significance to develop new techniques to determine trace uranyl ion in aqueous solution because it can induce severe threat to human health. We demonstrate here a novel strategy for recognition of uranyl ion with high selectivity and sensitivity by fluorescent polymer containing amidoxime groups. Specifically, the polymeric sensor is obtained by the amidoximation of poly(diimino 2,2-dicyanoethylene). There is a linear responsive range of 10 – 150nM and a fast response time of 200s for the detection of uranyl ions. The sensor shows the strong binding capability and the efficient quenching towards uranyl ions, and can be successfully used for detection of uranyl ion in environmental solution (such as lake water). This work indicates that fluorescent polymer with amidoxime groups may be a promising sensor of uranyl ion in aqueous solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amidoxime
Fluorescent polymer
Sensors
Photoluminescence
Uranyl ion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst otherkwds primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 13C NMR spectra (400MHz) of (A) P1, (B) P2 and (C) P3 in DMSO-d 6. (D) FT-IR spectra of (a) P1, (b) P2, and (c) P3.

(A) The fluorescent intensity variation spectra of P3 with different concentration (0.5~5.0×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units)) in DMA/H2O (95/5, v/v); (B) The response time curve of P3 (1.0×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units) to uranyl ions (1.5×10−5 mol L−1) at 547nm in DMA/H2O (95/5, v/v) solution at pH 6.0.

(A) The fluorescence spectra of P3 in DMA/H2O (95/5, v/v) with uranyl concentrations (experimental conditions: λex=340nm, [P3]=1×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units, uranyl concentrations: 0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.075, 0.1, 0.15, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 30, and 50×10–6 mol L−1); and (B) Plot of fluorescence quenching efficiencies (F0/F-1) as a function of uranyl concentrations, and the inset of linear Stern–Volmer at low concentrations: F0/F – 1=1.58×106 [UO2 2+], R2=0.986.
Fluorescence-quenching degrees of P3 in the presence of different metal ions (experimental conditions: [P3]=1×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units, and [metal cation]=1.5×10−5 mol L−1).
A competitive binding assay with P3 (1×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units) for UO2 2+(1.5×10−5 mol L−1) in the presence of other metal ions (1.5×10−5 mol L−1) in DMA/H2O (95/5, v/v).
Schematic depiction of the synthesis of polymer sensor and the possible structure of uranyl-amidoxime complex.
A comparison of the detection performance of sensors toward uranyl ions.
The results of sample measurements by P3 (1×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units) and ICP-MC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: It is from Dushu lake in Suzhou, China.

head.ce:title: Amidoximated fluorescent polymer based sensor for detection of trace uranyl ion in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School for Radiological and Interdisciplinary Sciences (RAD–X) & College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School for Radiological and Interdisciplinary Sciences (RAD–X) & College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Radiological Medicine of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions, Suzhou 215123, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Radiological Medicine of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School for Radiological and Interdisciplinary Sciences (RAD–X) & College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is of significance to develop new techniques to determine trace uranyl ion in aqueous solution because it can induce severe threat to human health. We demonstrate here a novel strategy for recognition of uranyl ion with high selectivity and sensitivity by fluorescent polymer containing amidoxime groups. Specifically, the polymeric sensor is obtained by the amidoximation of poly(diimino 2,2-dicyanoethylene). There is a linear responsive range of 10 – 150nM and a fast response time of 200s for the detection of uranyl ions. The sensor shows the strong binding capability and the efficient quenching towards uranyl ions, and can be successfully used for detection of uranyl ion in environmental solution (such as lake water). This work indicates that fluorescent polymer with amidoxime groups may be a promising sensor of uranyl ion in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium is an important element that has been widely used in nuclear power. With the development of nuclear industry, the possibility of human being exposed to trace amount of uranium is increasing. There is also growing concern that uranium can cause kidney damage [1] urinary system diseases and lung cancer [2]because of its radioactivity and chemical toxicity [3]. The maximum level of uranyl ions (UO2 2+) in drinking water is defined as 1.30×10−7 molL−1 by Environmental Protection Agency of the United States [4]. Therefore, it becomes the focus to develop a simple and convenient analytical method for on-site and rapid determination of uranyl ion in environmental solutions.

Currently, uranium detection relies primarily on instrumental analysis methods, such as surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [5], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [6,7], laser-induced kinetic phosphorimetry [8], and atomic absorption spectrometry [9]. However, all these measurements request complicated and expensive instruments, which makes real-time and on-site sensing difficult. In order to break the shackles of traditional instruments, chemical sensors have been designed through using various techniques including electrochemistry [10,11], colorimetry [12,13], and fluorescence [14,15]. Among these strategies, fluorescence detection possesses intrinsic sensitivity and high spatial resolution [16], which can satisfy the requirement of on-site and rapid detection of trace uranyl ion, thus attracting much attention in recent years.

In the past few years, fluorescence sensors of small-molecule were designed for the determination of uranyl ion [17,18]. Nevertheless, this sort of sensor is short of enough photo-stability and has low photo-luminescence efficiency [19]. In addition, Liu et al. reported a beacon sensor for detection of uranyl ions by using UO2 2+-specific DNAzyme with the detection limit of 45 pM and selectivity of >1-million-fold over other metal ion [20]. However, the UO2 2+-specific DNAzyme is expensive because of its complexity in vitro-selection. Very recently, our group reported a thermo-responsive polymeric sensor for uranyl ion with a stable response over a concentration range (1.0×10–7~1.0×10–3 mol L−1), but the selectivity is not high enough to meet the practical need [21]. It is still a challenge to develop new methods that possess high selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of trace uranyl ions in the actual environment.

Fluorescent polymer based sensors have been used for a lot of target detecting such as biomolecules [22], metal ions [23] and toxic chemicals [24], because of their remarkable sensing ability. Compared with small organic compounds, fluorescent polymer can effectively amplify the fluorescence signal via the energy migration or resonance energy transfer along the polymer backbone [25]. In addition, polymers are able to be easily fabricated, purified and modified [26]. Although plenty of fluorescent polymer sensors have been prepared for detection of various metal ions [27], there are few reports on the detection of UO2 2+ by utilizing fluorescent polymer sensors until now.

On the other hand, amidoxime group (–C(NH2)=N–OH) is one of the best groups to capture uranyl ions with high selectivity from aqueous solution (such as seawater or other natural water) [28–31]. Inspired by this finding, we report here a fluorescent polymer (P3) with amidoxime group for detection of uranyl ion ( Scheme 1). The diamidoxime structure endows the sensor with high probability of capturing trace uranyl ions via η2 coordination [32]. Meanwhile the polymer possesses good fluorescent properties due to multiple acceptor fluorophore. All of these advantages can help enhance the selectivity and detection limit of the sensor towards uranyl ion. Therefore, this work may provide a new strategy of highly selective and sensitive detection for trace uranyl ions.

Tris(dibenzylideneacetone)dipalladium(0) (Pd2(dba)3, AR), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR), N,N′-dimethylacetamide (DMA, AR) and 2,2’-bis-(diphenylphosphino)−1,1’-binaphthyl (BINAP, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Sodium tert-butoxide (AR), 1,4-bis-(4’-bromobenzoyl) benzene (AR), titanium isopropoxide (AR) and p-phenylenediamine (AR) were purchased from J & K Technology Co., Ltd. All other reagents were used as received.

Stock solutions of Ba2+, Ag+, Nd3+, Mn2+, Li+, K+, Mg2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Gd3+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, Dy3+, Sr2+, Ni+, Cs+ were prepared by adding nitrate salts into ultra-pure water from Millipore (Bedford, MA, USA). Uranyl nitrate (UO2(NO3)2 ▪ 6H2O, Fluka, AR) was dissolved for preparing the solution of uranium(VI). Hydroxylamine hydrochloride was adjusted to neutral by using NaOH solution (2.5molL−1). The characterization methods are listed in Supporting Information.

P1 was first synthesized for P3 synthesis (Supporting Information). P1 (0.986g, 3.5mmol) and malononitrile (0.462g, 7.0mmol) were dissolved in N, N′-dimethylformamide (DMF, 25mL), and then titanium isopropoxide (0.21mL, 0.7mmol) was injected into the solution. After stirring at 75°C for 72h under an argon atmosphere, the mixture was cooled down to room temperature and continued to stir for 0.5h in air condition to oxidize the catalyst. Then the mixture was filtered and the filtrate was precipitated in methanol (300mL). Finally the resultant solid were re-dissolved with DMF (20mL) and precipitated again in methanol (300mL). The intermediate（P2）was obtained as red powder (0.907g, 80.1% yield) by drying in a vacuum oven at 40°C.

Finally, hydroxylamine was used to react with the intermediate P2 to convert cyano group into amidoxime group. Specifically, P2 (0.528g, 1.64mmol) was mixed with hydroxylamine (0.541g, 16.4mmol) in DMF (24mL) and H2O/CH3OH (6.4mL; 9/1, v/v) at 75°C for 12h. The product (P3) was precipitated in 300mL methanol and filtered. The solid product (P3) was obtained as gray powder (0.473g, 74.3% yield) by drying in a vacuum oven at 30°C.

P3 were dissolved in DMA (100mL) under the stirring for 24h at room temperature to afford a stock solution (1.0×10–4 mol L−1 repeat units). Inorganic salt was prepared by dissolved in ultrapure water (100mL) to supply aqueous solution (1.0×10–3 mol L−1). The desired concentrations of stock solution was obtained by diluted with ultrapure water and the pH was adjusted to 6.0 by using HCl solution (1molL−1) when needed. Typically fluorescence and absorption experiments were carried out when aqueous solution (0.15mL) of metal salts was mixed with DMA solution of P3 (1.0×10–5 mol L−1 repeat units, 2.85mL) in a quartz cuvette and followed shaking for 5min.

The maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were first determined by UV–vis spectra of P3 (1.0×10–5 mol L−1 repeat units) in 3mL of DMA/H2O (95/5, v/v). Response time experiment was immediately recorded once every 50s when UO2 2+(1.5×10–5 mol L−1, 0.15mL) was added into P3 solution (1.0×10–5 mol L−1 repeat units, 2.85mL), and the total time of 300s was set up.

The solutions for recognizing UO2 2+ were prepared with P3 (1.0×10–5 mol L−1 repeat units). The different concentration of UO2 2+(i. e. 0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.075, 0.1, 0.15, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 15, 30, and 50×10–6 mol L−1) was obtained when appropriate aliquot of UO2 2+ stock solutions was added into the solution. The mixed solution of uranyl ion (1.5×10–5 mol L−1) and/or other ions (1.5×10–5 mol L−1) were prepared with P3 (1.0×10–5 mol L−1 repeat units) for the selective experiment.

To test the application of this sensor, lake water, tap water and deionized water (49mL) containing uranyl ion was prepared by adding 1mL of uranyl solution (1.0×10–4 mol L−1) [33]. The pH was adjusted to 6.0 by using HCl solution (1molL−1). Lake water was got from Dushu lake (Suzhou China) and filtered before use. Fluorescence experiment were carried out by adding 0.15mL sample solution to DMA solution of P3 (2.85mL) in a quartz cuvette after shaking for 5min.

The synthesis process of polymer sensor was shown in Scheme 1. The polymer P1 bearing carbonyl groups in main chain was first synthesized with Mn=9700g/mol and PDI=1.42 (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), and was reacted with malononitrile to bring in cyano groups. Afterwards, amidoxime groups were introduced by simple reaction with hydroxylamine to give the polymer sensor P3.

The chemical structure of polymer sensor was validated by 13C NMR and FT-IR spectra ( Fig. 1). The characteristic peak of keto group at δ=196ppm for P1 (Fig. 1A, peak a) was completely shifted to δ=177ppm (Fig. 1B, peak a) after reacting with malononitrile, while the characteristic peak of cyano group at δ=113ppm and 82ppm was shown for P2 (Fig. 1B, peak b and c). After amidoximation, the characteristic peaks were shifted to δ=167ppm, 160ppm and δ=120ppm for the carbon of P3, respectively (Fig. 1C). The result was further confirmed by 1H NMR spectra (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). The elemental analysis also indicated that P3 was successfully synthesized by this method (Table S1, Supporting Information).

The structures of the resultants were further confirmed by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1D). Compared with P1 (Fig. 1D, trace a), the characteristic bands occurred at 2214 and 1697cm−1 for –CN and -C˭C group of P2, respectively (Fig. 1C, trace b). After the reaction of P2 with hydroxylamine, the stretching band of –CN group was almost disappeared, while the bands of –C˭N, –C–N and –N–O of amidoxime group occurred at 1635, 1400 and 922cm−1, respectively (Fig. 1D, trace c). All the results indicated that P3 was successfully synthesized by this approach.

The fluorescence of the developed polymer probably comes from the conjugated aromatic diamidoxime unit. There is high affinity and selectivity for amidoxime group towards uranium (VI) sorption at about pH 6.0 [31,34]. Therefore, pH 6.0 was selected in the next measurements for fluorescence property. The maximum absorption wavelength (λabs) was first determined as 340nm at pH 6.0 (Fig. S3, Supporting Information). In order to determine maximum emission peak, the fluorescence of P3 was tested at different concentrations, and the result was shown in Fig. S4 (Supporting Information). The maximum emission peak was located at 547nm for P3. When the concentration beyond 1.0×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units, there was a slight red shift (0−6nm) owning to the intertwining between the molecules [35].

Fig. 2A shows fluorescence intensity at 547nm as a function of P3 concentration. Fluorescence intensity increased linearly with the concentrations below 1.0×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units, and then reached the maximum value at 2.5×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units. The result may be ascribed to self-quenching1 when polymer sensors at high concentrations (>1.0×10−5 mol L−1 repeat units). As a result, 1.0×10−5 mol L−1 of P3 was selected for fluorescent detection of metal ions. Moreover, the time stability of fluorescence intensity for P3 was investigated as shown in Fig. S5 (Supporting Information). Nearly no change of the fluorescence intensity could be seen after storing for 120 days, which indicated an excellent stability of the polymer.

A fast response process is very important for a fluorescent sensor to detect metal ions in actual environment. In order to investigate the response time of P3 to uranyl ions, the fluorescence intensity (λ=547nm) of P3 with uranyl ions was recorded over a period of 300s (Fig. 2B). The result indicated the stable reading could be obtained within 200s. The fast response time of the polymer-fluorescent toward UO2 2+ might be attributed to the strong coordination of amidoxime group in polymer chain.

In order to determine uranyl ions in aqueous solution, the influence of uranyl concentrations on fluorescence emission of P3 was studied. Fig. 3A shows the emission spectra of P3 with different concentrations of uranyl ions. The existence of uranyl ions resulted in a gradual quenching of P3 emission. A detection limit of 10nM and a maximum quenching of 65% were obtained. Stern–Volmer Eq. (1) can be used to describe the fluorescence quenching efficiency [36]:
(1)
F
0
/ F = 1 + Ksv
[ A ]
where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of quencher, and F is fluorescence intensity at the concentration of analyte quencher (i. e. [A]). Ksv is Stern–Volmer constant, and the bigger Ksv, the better quenching efficiency.

Fig. 3B shows (F0/F–1) as a function of the concentration of uranyl ions. At low concentrations of uranyl ions (<1.50×10−7 mol L−1), a linear Stern–Volmer plot was shown with a large Ksv of 1.58×106 molL−1, suggesting the efficient quenching of P3 by uranyl ions [37,38]. The detection range of uranyl ions is exactly consistent with the maximum allowable level of UO2 2+(1.30×10−7 mol L−1) defined by U. S. Environmental Protection Agency in drinking water [4]. The result indicated that amidoximated fluorescent polymers may provide a feasible approach to detect trace uranyl ions.

There are various metal ions existing in the environmental solution, and it is probable that these ions would interfere with the detection of UO2 2+. To clarify this point, the selectivity of P3 for UO2 2+ detection was explored. Some lanthanides [18] (such as Nd3+, Gd3+, and Dy3+) and common metal ions (such as Li+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, In+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+, Sr2+, Cs+ and Ni2+; 1.5×10−5 mol L−1) [39,40]were added into P3 solution (10−5 mol L−1 repeat units), respectively. The fluorescence intensity was measured for P3 solution (Fig. S6, Supporting Information).

Fluorescence-quenching degrees (1− F/F0) of P3 solution with different metal ions were shown in Fig. 4. When some metal ions (such as Li+, Ba2+, Sr2+, Cs+, Cr3+, Nd3+, Ca2+, Mg2+) was added into P3 solution, negative values (−10 ~ 0) of (1− F/F0) indicated these ions didn’t make any quenching effect. For the other metal ions (such as Zn2+, Gd3+, K+, Hg2+, Na+, Ag+, Cd2+, Dy3+, and In+), the fluorescence quenching efficiency was not significant ((1− F/F0) <5%) [18]. It was noticed that Ni2+ and Fe3+ made a small fluorescence quenching (10–14%), which were much lower than that of UO2 2+, which may be attributed to the good specificity of amidoxime group towards uranyl ion. The results suggested that P3 may be a potential fluorescent sensor for UO2 2+ with high selectivity.

In order to investigate whether coexisting metal ions would disturb the detection of UO2 2+, the experiment of uranyl determination was performed in the presence of each metal ion (such as Li+, Mg2+, Ba2+, In+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Fe3+,Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, K+, Sr2+, Na+, Cs+, Ni2+, Ag+, Nd3+, Gd3+, and Dy3+; 1.5×10–5 mol L−1), respectively. The quenching of fluorescence intensity of P3 by UO2 2+ with each coexisting ion was similar to sole UO2 2+(Fig. S7, Supporting Information).

Fb is the fluorescence intensity of P3 adding solely UO2 2+, while F′ stands for the fluorescence intensity of P3/UO2 2+ in the presence of coexisting ion. Therefore, the value of F′/Fb reflects the selectivity of P3 for UO2 2+ in the presence of other cations, and the result is depicted in Fig. 5. The negligible variation of F′/Fb value indicated that competing ions didn’t significantly change the quenching effect of uranyl ions. When the coexisting ions were tested with higher concentration, such as 1×10−3 mol L−1 for K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Li+, and Ag+; 1×10−4 mol L−1 for Cr3+, Zn2+, Nd3+, Gd3+, Dy3+, In+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Sr2+, and Cs+; 3×10−5 mol L−1 for Fe3+and Ni2+ in DMA/H2O (95/5, v/v), respectively. The similar results were obtained as shown in Fig. S8 and S9 (Supporting Information). These results may be attributed to the strongest binding capacity and best selectivity of amidoxime group for UO2 2+ among the cations [28–31], suggesting that P3 could be used for detecting uranyl ion in complex media.

The fluorescence quenched mechanism of P3 to UO2 2+ may be attributed to a photo-induced electron transfer process [41]. Upon UO2 2+ addition, the binding of P3 can lead to photo-induced electron transfer from P3 to UO2 2+, which can cause a perturbation of the electronic states of P3 as well as non-radiative transitions, thus leading to a significant fluorescence quenching [42,43].

A comparison of detection performance of sensors toward uranyl ions was made in the view of main materials, responsive time, detection range as well as the selectivity of sensors ( Table 1). Compared to the other sensors in the previous literatures, the sensor in this work is a competitive one which could be applied in practical usage in the future.

The detection of UO2 2+ in the environment was performed in the view of practical application. Lake water, tap water and deionized water were chosen to be solvents for preparing the solutions of uranyl ions (1.0×10−7 mol L−1). The uranyl concentration of the samples was determined by P3 and ICP-MS, respectively. The results are shown in Table 2. The uranyl concentrations of deionized water, lake water and tap water were detected by P3 as 0.98×10−7, 1.08×10−7 and 1.06×10−7 mol L−1, respectively. In comparison with the spiked amount in deionized water, acceptable deviation was obtained for lake water and tap water. Furthermore, the values could be comparable to the results from ICP-MS. The results indicated that the polymer sensor can effectively detect the concentration of UO2 2+ in environmental solution.

We demonstrate here a novel fluorescent polymer sensor containing amidoxime groups for determination of UO2 2+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The polymeric sensor was obtained by amidoximation of poly(diimino 2,2-dicyanoethylene), and showed stable optical properties and fine fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of 547nm (λex =340nm). Meanwhile, the sensor exhibited a linear responsive range of 10 – 150nM and a fast response time of 200s for uranyl ions through fluorescence “turn-off” response. Importantly, there was the high selectivity for the sensor binding with UO2 2+ in comparison with other ions, which could be used for detecting uranyl ion in lake water. The result indicates that the fluorescent polymer can be used as a promising sensor for effective detecting of UO2 2+ in environmental solutions.

To the best of our knowledge, the fluorescent polymer containing amidoxime group is utilized for the first time in detection of UO2 2+. The concept should be applicable to fluorescent polymer with the other ligands for detection of uranyl ions, which is helpful to design and synthesize more effective polymer sensors in future research.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (21504059, U1532111, 91326202), a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection, and China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M571811). J. Ma and W. He contributed equally.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2017.02.058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material: Characterization methods, synthesis and GPC curve of P1, element analysis of P1, P2 and P3, absorption and fluorescent spectra of P3 with/without metal ions at different concentrations. This material is available free of charge via the Internet at http://xxxxxxxxx.
.
